1: <header>Life cycle environmental impacts of using food waste liquid fodder as an alternative for pig feeding in a conventional Cuban farm </header>

Yasmani Alba-Reyes *,1 , Ernesto L. Barrera 1 , Yaima Brito-Ibarra, Félix Orestes Hermida-García Centre for Energy and Industrial Processes Studies (CEEPI), University of Sancti Spiritus, Ave de Los Martires 360, 60100 Sancti Spiritus, Cuba
2:
<list>Food waste liquid fodder impact benefi- cially on the environmental profile. 
Biogas from pig farms reduces fossil fuel in the food waste liquid fodder process. 
Identification of main contributors across different impact categories 
Avoiding a mineral synthetic fertilizer tributes favorably in the system boundary. 
Climate change is the most sensitive to protein changes in liquid fodder.</list>
4: Keywords: Life cycle assessment Pig feeding Food waste Waste management Anaerobic digestion
5: This work aimed to compare cleaner production alternatives for pig production in the Cuban context through the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach emphasizing the utilization of food waste (FW) as a substitute for traditional grain- based pig feeding. A conventional waste management method (lagooning) was assessed, including more environmen- tally friendly approaches (use of anaerobic digestion (AD) process); including the substitution of a fraction of solid fod- der with food waste liquid fodder (LF), obtained from food waste. The analysis was based on one porcine equivalent livestock unit. The environmental impact categories assessed were global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity, human car- cinogenic toxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, terrestrial acidification, and freshwater eutrophication. The major environ- mental benefits for pig production were observed when the maximum capacity of pigs was considered. In addition, favorable environmental performance was achieved by considering the substitution of solid fodder by LF, the AD as a waste management process, and the valorization of the solid and liquid effluents. The avoided products-related ac- tivities were the main contributor to freshwater ecotoxicity, human carcinogenic toxicity, and terrestrial ecotoxicity impact categories (up to 71%). The sensitivity analysis showed that the variation in LF composition (protein concen- tration) could have a remarkable impact in all impact categories. Climate change performed as the more sensible im- pact category, suggesting that greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, such as CO2 and N20, are important drivers to change the environmental impact and need more attention. This research demonstrates that the environmental profile of the process can be improved by applying a cleaner production approach (AD as a waste management alternative and LF substituting solid fodder).
6: Cuban pig sector is primarily focused on producing high-efficiency pigs in an intensive approach, providing a secure protein source to the national market. To raise support for domestic producers, the government has cre- ated incentives, bringing with it the increase in herds. Although pig meat
contributes to food security, it impacts the environment, causing damage to biodiversity, water degradation, and eutrophication (Folino et al., 2020; Ramirez-Islas et al., 2020).

Pig production is reported as a highly resource-consuming activity (Dou et al., 2018). Besides, it is associated with damages to the environment such as eutrophication, land use, acidification, and greenhouse gas emissions per kg of pork generated (Nguyen et al., 2012). Most of these impacts are as- sociated with the consumption of grains (Salemdeeb et al., 2017). Conse- quently, the substitution of grains in a pig diet can improve the environmental profile of pig production. The Cuban porcine sector is high- dependent on imported feed, increasing the costs and insecurity due to the volatility of prices in the international market (Martín-Casas et al., 2017).

Currently, efforts are made to guarantee an efficient supply from local feed sources, substituting part of these imported products (Cabrera et al., 2012). To secure the animal feed chain supply, alternative feed ingredients interest has been raised for the pig diet (Woyengo et al., 2014). However, its impacts on the environmental profile are not well studied (Mackenzie et al., 2016). The utilization of food waste (FW) represents a valorization of a high protein source, increasing animal feed self-sufficiency. Precursors of FW as animal feeding remark its use as a low-cost feed, and environmentally benign compared with grains (Stuart, 2009; Wadhwa and Bakshi, 2013). This statement is supported by Zu Ermgassen et al. (2016) which affirmed that 1.8 million ha focused on grain production can be reduced if all FW is valorized for porcine feed by current technologies. Another study carried out by Kim et al. (2014) reported the benefits of the environmental performance of re- placing feed grains with FW in the United States. A high amount of water (110-140 m³) and fertilizer (17 kg N) can be avoided if one tonne of maize grain is substituted. Besides, Wang et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2014) re- ported savings of 455-476 of water and 29 kg N of fertilizer in China.

Researchers and producers are suspicious of FW consumption in pig feeding since some sanitary incidents have occurred in the past (Dame- Korevaar al., 2021). It is reported that the main disadvantage of direct an- imal feeding is the risk of disease transmission and contamination of several pathogens (Dame-Korevaar et al., 2021). For that reason, some countries like the EU prohibit the use of FW for animal feeding (Zu Ermgassen et al., 2018). Cuban legislation is very clear, in large-scale farms is compul- sory boiling/cooking of FW before being fed to pigs, avoiding potential risks (Peña et al., 2013). Such farms must follow very strict rules in FW management practices and biosecurity.

The world trend is to increase the number of animals per farm, which is associated with a huge amount of waste (Lee and Han, 2012). Pig production generates a high abundance of manure and wastewater. Proper disposal of pig residuals poses big challenges in Cuba (GOC, 1997). Anaerobic digestion (AD) is identified as an attractive technology to manage pig manure (Ardebili, 2020; Yao et al., 2020), contributing to energy recovery and circular econ- omy, as well as the mitigation of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions throughout the pig life cycle (Zhu et al., 2019). AD is recognized in Cuba as a well- established technology due to its efficiency, economic feasibility, and the pos- sibility of managing different waste streams. However, not all pig farms pro- cess pig manure with this technology, generally treated by lagooning (Alba- Reyes et al., 2019; Gutiérrez et al., 2016) generating uncontrollable emissions of undigested substances and effluents with high organic matter concentra- tion (Folino et al., 2020). Currently, the application of medium-scale biogas production technologies (covered lagoons and hybrid technologies) using pig waste has increased considerably with the main objective to use this en- ergy carrier to generate electricity and thermal energy (Mittal et al., 2018). Moreover, valorizing biogas as a high-caloric energy carrier could impact beneficially not only on the environmental profile of the process but also in the profitability in general (Garfí et al., 2016; Rupf et al., 2015).

Within this framework, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is identified as a strong tool to evaluate the environmental performance of bioenergy sys- items (Lijó et al., 2017). Several authors remarked on the possibilities of LCA as a useful tool to assess the environmental profile of products, pro- cesses, and services (Dewulf and Van Langenhove, 2002; Dewulf et al., 2000). Besides, it considers the evaluation of different technological op- tions, and their potential effects on population and ecosystems (Dewulf

and Van Langenhove, 2002). Specifically, pig production is a well-study process according to the LCA approach (Pexas al., 2021), covering studies in Asia, Europe, and America (Esteves et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Ottosen et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2021). Using LCA to demonstrate the potential im- pacts of pig production can be useful for farmers, stakeholders, and decision-makers to reduce the potential impacts on nature.

There are reported several studies of LCA based on pig production, how- ever many difficulties related to the functional unit, scope, and system bound- aries are considered challenges (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Gutiérrez et al. (2016) assessed the impacts of the production of 120 kg live-weight pigs in Cienfuegos (Cuba). To improve its environmental profile, waste management processes must be improved (lagoons), and the author suggested the possibil- ity of direct land application considering local conditions. Alba-Reyes et al. (2019) compared three pig production technologies (Genetic, Multiplier, and Production farms) in the Cuban context. The authors proposed the imple- mentation of cleaner production alternatives due to higher impacts on cli- mate change, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, and terrestrial ecotoxicity compared with previous pig produc- tion technologies. This behavior is associated with a higher amount of feed and enteric gas generation per functional unit.

Some studies in the literature are focused on the LCA of FW as a nutri- tional option. Salemdeeb et al. (2017) compared four technologies through- out the LCA approach, two of them were concentrated on FW used as animal feed. The results showed that the processing of wet feed has the best environmental profile in 13 of 14 studied impact categories, mainly for the substitution of traditional feed. Zu Ermgassen et al. (2016) affirmed that the substitution of conventional pig feed (grains) by swill not only im- pacts beneficially in saving GHG emissions and decreasing eutrophication but can reduce land use. According to Vandermeersch et al. (2014), a study carried out in Belgium, using FW as animal feed, presented better environmental results compared with its use as a fuel in the AD process 26%). The authors declared that this performance is related to the consid- eration of avoided products from the traditional supply chain. Besides, lower emissions of GHG per unit of the protein content of substituted prod- ucts were obtained. However, in a study performed in Sweden, using FW in different scenarios, the authors obtained higher emissions compared with its use in AD (Eriksson et al., 2015).

In a study focused on global warming carried out in South Korea by Kim et al. (2011), the researchers affirmed that the use of FW as wet feeding can be considered an attractive option for reducing the emissions of 283 CO2- eq. Kim and Kim (2010) concluded that compared with other waste man- agement technologies (dry feeding, composting, and landfilling), the use of FW as wet food can cause lower impacts on global warming potential, emitting only 61 kg CO2-eq. However, little work has been carried out to as- sess the environmental performance by the LCA approach of the combina- tion of the substitution of pig feed grain by FW (to obtain boiled food waste liquid fodder (hereinafter LF)) and the replacement of fossil fuel (die- sel) by biogas obtained in pig waste management process (AD).

This study aims to address the environmental impacts of the life cycle of using food waste liquid fodder as an alternative for pig feeding in a conven- tional Cuban farm (TPFF). The impact of the substitution of solid fodder by LF and the substitution of fossil fuel (diesel) by the biogas generated by pig manure AD was considered with cleaner production alternatives. The envi- ronmental significance of the main activities involved in the whole life cycle of the selected system boundaries was evaluated. For the best sce- nario, sensitivity analysis was conducted to identify the influence of a crit- ical parameter in liquid fodder composition (protein). It is expected that the result of this study provides insights in terms of the perspective of sustain- able food security and waste management strategies in conventional pig production farms in developing countries.

Currently, the application of medium-scale biogas production technologies (covered lagoons and hybrid technologies) using pig waste has increased considerably with the main objective to use this en- ergy carrier to generate electricity and thermal energy (Mittal et al., 2018). Moreover, valorizing biogas as a high-caloric energy carrier could impact beneficially not only on the environmental profile of the process but also in the profitability in general (Garfí et al., 2016; Rupf et al., 2015).

Within this framework, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is identified as a strong tool to evaluate the environmental performance of bioenergy sys- items (Lijó et al., 2017). Several authors remarked on the possibilities of LCA as a useful tool to assess the environmental profile of products, pro- cesses, and services (Dewulf and Van Langenhove, 2002; Dewulf et al., 2000). Besides, it considers the evaluation of different technological op- tions, and their potential effects on population and ecosystems (Dewulf

and Van Langenhove, 2002). Specifically, pig production is a well-study process according to the LCA approach (Pexas al., 2021), covering studies in Asia, Europe, and America (Esteves et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Ottosen et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2021). Using LCA to demonstrate the potential im- pacts of pig production can be useful for farmers, stakeholders, and decision-makers to reduce the potential impacts on nature.

There are reported several studies of LCA based on pig production, how- ever many difficulties related to the functional unit, scope, and system bound- aries are considered challenges (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Gutiérrez et al. (2016) assessed the impacts of the production of 120 kg live-weight pigs in Cienfuegos (Cuba). To improve its environmental profile, waste management processes must be improved (lagoons), and the author suggested the possibil- ity of direct land application considering local conditions. Alba-Reyes et al. (2019) compared three pig production technologies (Genetic, Multiplier, and Production farms) in the Cuban context. The authors proposed the imple- mentation of cleaner production alternatives due to higher impacts on cli- mate change, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, and terrestrial ecotoxicity compared with previous pig produc- tion technologies. This behavior is associated with a higher amount of feed and enteric gas generation per functional unit.

Some studies in the literature are focused on the LCA of FW as a nutri- tional option. Salemdeeb et al. (2017) compared four technologies through- out the LCA approach, two of them were concentrated on FW used as animal feed. The results showed that the processing of wet feed has the best environmental profile in 13 of 14 studied impact categories, mainly for the substitution of traditional feed. Zu Ermgassen et al. (2016) affirmed that the substitution of conventional pig feed (grains) by swill not only im- pacts beneficially in saving GHG emissions and decreasing eutrophication but can reduce land use. According to Vandermeersch et al. (2014), a study carried out in Belgium, using FW as animal feed, presented better environmental results compared with its use as a fuel in the AD process 26%). The authors declared that this performance is related to the consid- eration of avoided products from the traditional supply chain. Besides, lower emissions of GHG per unit of the protein content of substituted prod- ucts were obtained. However, in a study performed in Sweden, using FW in different scenarios, the authors obtained higher emissions compared with its use in AD (Eriksson et al., 2015).

In a study focused on global warming carried out in South Korea by Kim et al. (2011), the researchers affirmed that the use of FW as wet feeding can be considered an attractive option for reducing the emissions of 283 CO2- eq. Kim and Kim (2010) concluded that compared with other waste man- agement technologies (dry feeding, composting, and landfilling), the use of FW as wet food can cause lower impacts on global warming potential, emitting only 61 kg CO2-eq. However, little work has been carried out to as- sess the environmental performance by the LCA approach of the combina- tion of the substitution of pig feed grain by FW (to obtain boiled food waste liquid fodder (hereinafter LF)) and the replacement of fossil fuel (die- sel) by biogas obtained in pig waste management process (AD).

This study aims to address the environmental impacts of the life cycle of using food waste liquid fodder as an alternative for pig feeding in a conven- tional Cuban farm (TPFF). The impact of the substitution of solid fodder by LF and the substitution of fossil fuel (diesel) by the biogas generated by pig manure AD was considered with cleaner production alternatives. The envi- ronmental significance of the main activities involved in the whole life cycle of the selected system boundaries was evaluated. For the best sce- nario, sensitivity analysis was conducted to identify the influence of a crit- ical parameter in liquid fodder composition (protein). It is expected that the result of this study provides insights in terms of the perspective of sustain- able food security and waste management strategies in conventional pig production farms in developing countries.
10: Cuban pig sector is primarily focused on producing high-efficiency pigs in an intensive approach, providing a secure protein source to the national market. To raise support for domestic producers, the government has cre- ated incentives, bringing with it the increase in herds. Although pig meat
contributes to food security, it impacts the environment, causing damage to biodiversity, water degradation, and eutrophication (Folino et al., 2020; Ramirez-Islas et al., 2020).

Pig production is reported as a highly resource-consuming activity (Dou et al., 2018). Besides, it is associated with damages to the environment such as eutrophication, land use, acidification, and greenhouse gas emissions per kg of pork generated (Nguyen et al., 2012). Most of these impacts are as- sociated with the consumption of grains (Salemdeeb et al., 2017). Conse- quently, the substitution of grains in a pig diet can improve the environmental profile of pig production. The Cuban porcine sector is high- dependent on imported feed, increasing the costs and insecurity due to the volatility of prices in the international market (Martín-Casas et al., 2017).

Currently, efforts are made to guarantee an efficient supply from local feed sources, substituting part of these imported products (Cabrera et al., 2012). To secure the animal feed chain supply, alternative feed ingredients interest has been raised for the pig diet (Woyengo et al., 2014). However, its impacts on the environmental profile are not well studied (Mackenzie et al., 2016). The utilization of food waste (FW) represents a valorization of a high protein source, increasing animal feed self-sufficiency. Precursors of FW as animal feeding remark its use as a low-cost feed, and environmentally benign compared with grains (Stuart, 2009; Wadhwa and Bakshi, 2013). This statement is supported by Zu Ermgassen et al. (2016) which affirmed that 1.8 million ha focused on grain production can be reduced if all FW is valorized for porcine feed by current technologies. Another study carried out by Kim et al. (2014) reported the benefits of the environmental performance of re- placing feed grains with FW in the United States. A high amount of water (110-140 m³) and fertilizer (17 kg N) can be avoided if one tonne of maize grain is substituted. Besides, Wang et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2014) re- ported savings of 455-476 of water and 29 kg N of fertilizer in China.

Researchers and producers are suspicious of FW consumption in pig feeding since some sanitary incidents have occurred in the past (Dame- Korevaar al., 2021). It is reported that the main disadvantage of direct an- imal feeding is the risk of disease transmission and contamination of several pathogens (Dame-Korevaar et al., 2021). For that reason, some countries like the EU prohibit the use of FW for animal feeding (Zu Ermgassen et al., 2018). Cuban legislation is very clear, in large-scale farms is compul- sory boiling/cooking of FW before being fed to pigs, avoiding potential risks (Peña et al., 2013). Such farms must follow very strict rules in FW management practices and biosecurity.

Currently, the application of medium-scale biogas production technologies (covered lagoons and hybrid technologies) using pig waste has increased considerably with the main objective to use this en- ergy carrier to generate electricity and thermal energy (Mittal et al., 2018). Moreover, valorizing biogas as a high-caloric energy carrier could impact beneficially not only on the environmental profile of the process but also in the profitability in general (Garfí et al., 2016; Rupf et al., 2015).

This behavior is associated with a higher amount of feed and enteric gas generation per functional unit.

To secure the animal feed chain supply, alternative feed ingredients interest has been raised for the pig diet (Woyengo et al., 2014). However, its impacts on the environmental profile are not well studied (Mackenzie et al., 2016). The utilization of food waste (FW) represents a valorization of a high protein source, increasing animal feed self-sufficiency. Precursors of FW as animal feeding remark its use as a low-cost feed, and environmentally benign compared with grains (Stuart, 2009; Wadhwa and Bakshi, 2013). This statement is supported by Zu Ermgassen et al. (2016) which affirmed that 1.8 million ha focused on grain production can be reduced if all FW is valorized for porcine feed by current technologies. Another study carried out by Kim et al. (2014) reported the benefits of the environmental performance of re- placing feed grains with FW in the United States. A high amount of water (110-140 m³) and fertilizer (17 kg N) can be avoided if one tonne of maize grain is substituted. Besides, Wang et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2014) re- ported savings of 455-476 of water and 29 kg N of fertilizer in China.

Researchers and producers are suspicious of FW consumption in pig feeding since some sanitary incidents have occurred in the past (Dame- Korevaar al., 2021). It is reported that the main disadvantage of direct an- imal feeding is the risk of disease transmission and contamination of several pathogens (Dame-Korevaar et al., 2021). For that reason, some countries like the EU prohibit the use of FW for animal feeding (Zu Ermgassen et al., 2018). Cuban legislation is very clear, in large-scale farms is compul- sory boiling/cooking of FW before being fed to pigs, avoiding potential risks (Peña et al., 2013). Such farms must follow very strict rules in FW management practices and biosecurity.

The world trend is to increase the number of animals per farm, which is associated with a huge amount of waste (Lee and Han, 2012). Pig production generates a high abundance of manure and wastewater. Proper disposal of pig residuals poses big challenges in Cuba (GOC, 1997). Anaerobic digestion (AD) is identified as an attractive technology to manage pig manure (Ardebili, 2020; Yao et al., 2020), contributing to energy recovery and circular econ- omy, as well as the mitigation of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions throughout the pig life cycle (Zhu et al., 2019). AD is recognized in Cuba as a well- established technology due to its efficiency, economic feasibility, and the pos- sibility of managing different waste streams. However, not all pig farms pro- cess pig manure with this technology, generally treated by lagooning (Alba- Reyes et al., 2019; Gutiérrez et al., 2016) generating uncontrollable emissions of undigested substances and effluents with high organic matter concentra- tion (Folino et al., 2020). Currently, the application of medium-scale biogas production technologies (covered lagoons and hybrid technologies) using pig waste has increased considerably with the main objective to use this en- ergy carrier to generate electricity and thermal energy (Mittal et al., 2018). Moreover, valorizing biogas as a high-caloric energy carrier could impact beneficially not only on the environmental profile of the process but also in the profitability in general (Garfí et al., 2016; Rupf et al., 2015).

Within this framework, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is identified as a strong tool to evaluate the environmental performance of bioenergy sys- items (Lijó et al., 2017). Several authors remarked on the possibilities of LCA as a useful tool to assess the environmental profile of products, pro- cesses, and services (Dewulf and Van Langenhove, 2002; Dewulf et al., 2000). Besides, it considers the evaluation of different technological op- tions, and their potential effects on population and ecosystems (Dewulf

and Van Langenhove, 2002). Specifically, pig production is a well-study process according to the LCA approach (Pexas al., 2021), covering studies in Asia, Europe, and America (Esteves et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Ottosen et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2021). Using LCA to demonstrate the potential im- pacts of pig production can be useful for farmers, stakeholders, and decision-makers to reduce the potential impacts on nature.

There are reported several studies of LCA based on pig production, how- ever many difficulties related to the functional unit, scope, and system bound- aries are considered challenges (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Gutiérrez et al. (2016) assessed the impacts of the production of 120 kg live-weight pigs in Cienfuegos (Cuba). To improve its environmental profile, waste management processes must be improved (lagoons), and the author suggested the possibil- ity of direct land application considering local conditions. Alba-Reyes et al. (2019) compared three pig production technologies (Genetic, Multiplier, and Production farms) in the Cuban context. The authors proposed the imple- mentation of cleaner production alternatives due to higher impacts on cli- mate change, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, and terrestrial ecotoxicity compared with previous pig produc- tion technologies. This behavior is associated with a higher amount of feed and enteric gas generation per functional unit.

Some studies in the literature are focused on the LCA of FW as a nutri- tional option. Salemdeeb et al. (2017) compared four technologies through- out the LCA approach, two of them were concentrated on FW used as animal feed. The results showed that the processing of wet feed has the best environmental profile in 13 of 14 studied impact categories, mainly for the substitution of traditional feed. Zu Ermgassen et al. (2016) affirmed that the substitution of conventional pig feed (grains) by swill not only im- pacts beneficially in saving GHG emissions and decreasing eutrophication but can reduce land use. According to Vandermeersch et al. (2014), a study carried out in Belgium, using FW as animal feed, presented better environmental results compared with its use as a fuel in the AD process 26%). The authors declared that this performance is related to the consid- eration of avoided products from the traditional supply chain. Besides, lower emissions of GHG per unit of the protein content of substituted prod- ucts were obtained. However, in a study performed in Sweden, using FW in different scenarios, the authors obtained higher emissions compared with its use in AD (Eriksson et al., 2015).

In a study focused on global warming carried out in South Korea by Kim et al. (2011), the researchers affirmed that the use of FW as wet feeding can be considered an attractive option for reducing the emissions of 283 CO2- eq. Kim and Kim (2010) concluded that compared with other waste man- agement technologies (dry feeding, composting, and landfilling), the use of FW as wet food can cause lower impacts on global warming potential, emitting only 61 kg CO2-eq. However, little work has been carried out to as- sess the environmental performance by the LCA approach of the combina- tion of the substitution of pig feed grain by FW (to obtain boiled food waste liquid fodder (hereinafter LF)) and the replacement of fossil fuel (die- sel) by biogas obtained in pig waste management process (AD).

This study aims to address the environmental impacts of the life cycle of using food waste liquid fodder as an alternative for pig feeding in a conven- tional Cuban farm (TPFF). The impact of the substitution of solid fodder by LF and the substitution of fossil fuel (diesel) by the biogas generated by pig manure AD was considered with cleaner production alternatives. The envi- ronmental significance of the main activities involved in the whole life cycle of the selected system boundaries was evaluated. For the best sce- nario, sensitivity analysis was conducted to identify the influence of a crit- ical parameter in liquid fodder composition (protein). It is expected that the result of this study provides insights in terms of the perspective of sustain- able food security and waste management strategies in conventional pig production farms in developing countries.

15: In the current study, the LCA methodology was applied to assess the environmental impact of a Cuban TPFF, located in the North-center region of the country (baseline case) in comparison with alternative scenarios,
considering a cleaner production approach. Besides, activities related to the process were studied, and a sensitivity analysis was made.
16: The goal of the present study was to assess the environmental impact of the TPFF (Cuba), through an LCA approach. It was considered improve- ment alternatives and future projections of the farm (increasing the number of pigs), focused on the substitution of solid fodder by LF and biogas pro- duction from the AD process (as a waste management process) for replacing fossil fuel. The analysis follows the concept of cradle-to-gate. Five alterna- tives were compared to determine the sustainability of cleaner production options in terms of environmental impacts.
17: The main function of the TPFF is to produce live-weight pigs for slaugh- ter. Piglets from pig breeding (outside the system boundary) are acquired weighing up to 13 kg and fattened up to 95 kg within 101 days. For that rea- son, it was considered as a functional unit the production of 50 kg as a por- cine equivalent livestock unit (PELU) of live pigs (weight average between all growing stages: pre-fattening, piglets, and fattening piglets, including the death rate of each stage) during one year. A similar functional unit was considered by Alba-Reyes et al. (2019).

The study was performed using the cradle-to-gate perspective, there- fore, stages such as pig feed transportation from abroad (corn and soybean), slaughtering, distribution, and pig meat consumption were excluded. Be- sides, environmental impacts related to infrastructure were omitted from the studied system. The different flows involved in the system under study are shown in Fig. 1.

Solid fodder included the main components of the pig diet (corn and soybean). Water consumption contemplated the cleaning water. Electricity consumption is referred to as lighting and water pumping (including the pumping during biogas plant operation), and fodder transportation (solid fodder and FW) is considered diesel consumption from outside the farm, as well as LF production. Biogas production and lagooning were considered technologies for waste management. For LF production, it was contem- plated that the use of biogas from AD and diesel for FW cooking, depending on the studied alternative. FW comes from hotel facilities located in Santa Maria Keys.

Pig feces are treated in systems that combine biogas production (AD) and/or lagooning, being degraded in lagooning just when the residues are not considered for biogas production. The content of phosphorous (P) in wastewater (from lagooning) and digestate are considered known outputs to the technosphere, as well as wastewater for crop irrigation, are consid- ered avoided products. Corn, soybean, and charcoal (biogas cleaning unit) are considered supplies from the technosphere (materials). Water con- sumption resources are taken from nature (ecosphere). Electricity and

A-1 Imported pig feed only, 4000 pigs herd, no AD A-2 Imported pig feed/LF, 4000 pigs herd, no AD A-3 Imported pig feed/LF, 4000 pigs herd, diesel substitued by biogas from AD A-4 Imported pig feed/LF, 8000 pigs herd, diesel substitued by biogas from AD, maximum LF plant capacity A-5 Imported pig feed/LF, 8000 pigs herd, diesel substitued by biogas from AD, average LF plant capacity

diesel are taken from the technosphere. Diesel and biogas combustion gases are emissions to the air (ecosphere). Both, Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) and Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD5) are considered emissions to water (ecosphere), as well as water in digestate. Enteric emissions from pig housing are considered emissions to the air (ecosphere), and the components identified in digestate are considered emissions to the soil (ecosphere).

20: <header>2.3. Description of the system under study </header>

The present study was carried out in a TPFF "Texico" located in Caibarién (79.5175 long. W; 22.5138 lat. N), Villa Clara province. The farm comprises 4000 pigs, using pre-fattening pigs with 13 kg from genetic farms outside the facility. The main objective of the current farm is to pro- duce pigs ready for slaughtering (101 days cycle and 95 kg as exit weight) in three main stages: pre-fattening, piglets, and fattening piglets. It operates with 2.5 cycles/year, with a death rate of 3% (Peña et al., 2013) (Table 1).

Pig production characteristics in Cuba involve several stages (Alba- Reyes et al., 2019), which are related to solid fodder transportation from the port and distribution to the farm, pig housing (pre-fattening, piglets, and fattening piglets), lagooning, LF cooking, and biogas generation de- pending on the alternative considered. All emissions to soil, water, and air as a consequence of the process under study are considered within the system boundary. The key parameters and the main assumptions in the analysis concerning all the processes are described below.
21: Pig diet in Cuba is essentially corn and soybean (up to 80 %) (Gutiérrez et al., 2016; Martín-Casas et al., 2017), coming from the United States and Brazil, respectively (Alba-Reyes et al., 2019). There are reported other com- ponents in the pig diet (NRC, 2012), however, since their concentration is insignificant, they were not considered for the current study. Amid volatil- ity and excessive increment in typical solid fodder for pigs (Ali et al., 2017), as well as the concern for the environmental impacts of grains for animal feeding (e.g. corn and soybean) (Nguyen et al., 2012), there is growing in- terest to potentiate the use of alternative animal feed. FW is considered a conventional pig feed, however, some components like meat are reported as the main cause of transmitting diseases like African swine fever, and foot-and-mouth disease (Salemdeeb et al., 2017). To prevent such prob- lems, heat treatment is recommended (García et al., 2005). It is suggested that if the European Union recycled the same proportion of FW as some Asian countries (e.g., Japan and Korea), it could impact beneficially on the environment, reducing land use for pork production by 1.8 million hect- ares of farmland (Zu Ermgassen et al., 2016). In addition, some references support the inclusion of FW in pig feed with no significant effect on the or- ganoleptic quality of the pork meat, reaching an adequate feed conversion (Márquez and Ramos, 2007; Widayati et al., 2019).

In Cuba, the use of FW as a substitute for solid fodder for pig feeding is promoted. However, a thermal treatment before pig feeding is a universal requirement and necessary to achieve a product with sanitary, microbiolog- ical, and nutritional parameters suitable for animal needs. There are re- ported different parameters to obtain LF from FW processing. Westendorf et al. (1998) analyzed FW at 100 °C for 4 h for pig feeding. García et al. (2005) processed FW, heated in a range of 65-80 °C for 10-60 min. It is re- ported for FW thermal treatment, parameters like up to 65 °C and 20 min (Sancho et al., 2004), however, one-hour cycles for the current case are assessed according to the installed technology.

FW is reported as a variable mixture of nutrients over time, with a coef- ficient of variation in some components that can reach up to 100 9 (Westendorf, 2000). Variations in FW composition impact directly pig diet, affecting potential weight gain during its different growth stages. However, for a better understanding of FW use as a pig feed, it was assumed that post-consumption hotels FW was reported by Ho and Chu (2019). FW is collected from 22 hotels in Santa María Keys area. For the present study, FW is screened to avoid solid particles like bones and metals from the
Table 1 Characteristics of pig production of a TPFF.
Growing stages|Input-output weight (kg)|Duration (d)|Death rate (%) Pre-fattening|13-50|54|3 Piglet|50-90|41|3 Fattening piglets|90-95|6|3
kitchen. For transportation, the containers are cooled to avoid fermenta- tion.

FW is a heterogeneous mix of proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates, with optimum moisture and pH range between 55 and 65 % and 5.5-8.0 respec- tively (Christensen, 2011; Diaz et al., 2011). Protein in FW is reported lower than the limit for pig feeding, providing insufficient dietary support, and being necessary supplementation with solid fodder for the complete animal cycle (Ho and Chu, 2019). Dietary requirements following the physiologi- cal ages of pigs are shown in Table 2.

It is re- ported for FW thermal treatment, parameters like up to 65 °C and 20 min (Sancho et al., 2004), however, one-hour cycles for the current case are assessed according to the installed technology.

FW is reported as a variable mixture of nutrients over time, with a coef- ficient of variation in some components that can reach up to 100 9 (Westendorf, 2000). Variations in FW composition impact directly pig diet, affecting potential weight gain during its different growth stages. However, for a better understanding of FW use as a pig feed, it was assumed that post-consumption hotels FW was reported by Ho and Chu (2019). FW is collected from 22 hotels in Santa María Keys area. FW is a heterogeneous mix of proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates, with optimum moisture and pH range between 55 and 65 % and 5.5-8.0 respec- tively (Christensen, 2011; Diaz et al., 2011). Protein in FW is reported lower than the limit for pig feeding, providing insufficient dietary support, and being necessary supplementation with solid fodder for the complete animal cycle (Ho and Chu, 2019). Dietary requirements following the physiologi- cal ages of pigs are shown in Table 2.
23: Pig diet in Cuba is essentially corn and soybean (up to 80 %) (Gutiérrez et al., 2016; Martín-Casas et al., 2017), coming from the United States and Brazil, respectively (Alba-Reyes et al., 2019). There are reported other com- ponents in the pig diet (NRC, 2012), however, since their concentration is insignificant, they were not considered for the current study. Amid volatil- ity and excessive increment in typical solid fodder for pigs (Ali et al., 2017), as well as the concern for the environmental impacts of grains for animal feeding (e.g. corn and soybean) (Nguyen et al., 2012), there is growing in- terest to potentiate the use of alternative animal feed. FW is considered a conventional pig feed, however, some components like meat are reported as the main cause of transmitting diseases like African swine fever, and foot-and-mouth disease (Salemdeeb et al., 2017). To prevent such prob- lems, heat treatment is recommended (García et al., 2005). It is suggested that if the European Union recycled the same proportion of FW as some Asian countries (e.g., Japan and Korea), it could impact beneficially on the environment, reducing land use for pork production by 1.8 million hect- ares of farmland (Zu Ermgassen et al., 2016). In addition, some references support the inclusion of FW in pig feed with no significant effect on the or- ganoleptic quality of the pork meat, reaching an adequate feed conversion (Márquez and Ramos, 2007; Widayati et al., 2019).

In Cuba, the use of FW as a substitute for solid fodder for pig feeding is promoted. However, a thermal treatment before pig feeding is a universal requirement and necessary to achieve a product with sanitary, microbiolog- ical, and nutritional parameters suitable for animal needs. There are re- ported different parameters to obtain LF from FW processing. Westendorf et al. (1998) analyzed FW at 100 °C for 4 h for pig feeding. García et al. (2005) processed FW, heated in a range of 65-80 °C for 10-60 min. It is re- ported for FW thermal treatment, parameters like up to 65 °C and 20 min (Sancho et al., 2004), however, one-hour cycles for the current case are assessed according to the installed technology.

25: This distance covered by trucks from the fattening farm and the nearest port (Cienfuegos) is 122 km (one-way), but diesel consumption and emis- sions were calculated in a round-trip distance. The same assumption was made in hotels-to-farm transportation. Due to the 22 hotels that supply FW being spread in a particular area (Santa María Keys), the destination of a particular place is difficult to identify. For that reason, the average dis- tance from every hotel to the farm was made (61 km). Regarding pig feed transportation, larger quantities imply a greater consumption of fuel, which impacts the inventory of each alternative under study (Table 3). Diesel-fueled Kamaz truck was assumed for the transportation of both FW and solid fodder, with a diesel consumption index of 24-60 L, and empty- full capacity, respectively (Bonzon et al., 2005). Emissions from diesel- fueled trucks (CO, COV, NO2, SO2, and PM < 10) were modeled according to Mollenhauer et al. (2010).
26: The main goal of this stage is the production of 95 kg of live-weight pigs within 101 days, obtained from 13 kg piglets (Peña et al., 2013). As main inputs for pig production include conventional fodder (corn and soybean), and LF, depending on the alternative considered. Drinking and cleaning water, electricity for pumping, and illumination were also included in the inventory. Table 1 shows the main characteristics of pig production in a TPFF. Hormones, antibiotics, chemical cleaning agents, and pathogens emissions were excluded from the present study.

Pig manure generation is modeled as 5 % of pig live-weight (Alba-Reyes et al., 2019), with 20 of volatile solids (Zarkadas and Pilidis, 2011). En- teric fermentation from pig housing (volume of gases emitted by pigs phys- iologically) was constant during the study. These emissions were estimated using the method declared by Stone et al. (2012), and they were based on data provided by Dalgaard et al. (2007) (CH4 and NH3), and Dong et al. (2007) (CO2).

For energy consumption (electricity and solid fodder transportation), own measurements were made. Electricity requirements for pig lighting and water pumping were considered. The electricity consumption was modeled considering the national electricity mix configuration: centralized and decentralized thermal power plants (83.68 combined cycles with

The incineration of dead pigs is widely used in Cuban pig farms as a waste management method. Emissions of NO2 and CO2 are considered the most representative, regarding the meat: bones pigs ratio, amount of water in meat, proteins, lipids, and ashes (Heinz and Hautzinger, 2007).
30: Manure management is referred to as treating pig feces. Lagooning is recognized as the most used waste management technology in Cuba (Gutiérrez et al., 2016). It is assumed a conventional anaerobic-aerobic facultative lagooning treatment with COD and BOD5 removals by 64.2 o and 67.2 respectively (Sánchez-Morales et al., 2011). BOD5/COD ratio was assumed as 43.5 %, and the model used for COD composition in the lagooning effluent was proposed by Toprak (1995) and Wu and Chen (2011). Besides, a 50 % efficiency in organic matter removal is assumed in a typical pig waste lagoon (Toprak, 1995).

AD is a mature technology for managing pig waste (Zhi et al., 2021). Cuban Hybrid Technology (CHT) is a widespread technology that allows a reduction of organic matter of different diluted or concentrated residues (sugarcane, bovines, porcine, and vegetable residues). It is recognized >200 AD plants spread throughout the country, being flexible on a domes- tic scale (3-50 m³ reactor) as well as industrial scale (up to 763 m³ reactor). CHT consists of masonry rectangular technology and is covered by a rein- forced concrete structure. The digester is usually made of blocks or prefabricated structures, with vertical walls inside as a labyrinth, with a horizontal circulation geometry, similar to upflow anaerobic sludge blanket (UASB) reactor (Hermida García, 2018).

It is reported the rate of treating residues of 10 pigs/m3 reactor, is higher than fixed dome digesters (3 pigs/m r reactor). Compared with other technologies in Cuba, CHT reached a gas volumetric productivity be- tween 1 and 1.2 m³ biogas/m² digester/d (Hermida García et al., 2020), higher than other AD technologies reported in the country (e.g. fixed dome digester, floating drum digester, and inflatable tubular digester) (Hermida García, 2014). To ensure good contact between microorganisms and substrate, it is established a residual velocity limit of around 10 m/h

(Hermida García et al., 2020). Besides, it is reported an organic load re- moval of up to 60 % of COD input, and a hydraulic retention time of 2.5 d, reaching a volumetric organic load of up to 6 kg volatile solids/m³/ Biogas quality reaches 60 % methane in a continuous regime (Jiménez et al., 2015) (the rest corresponds to CO2 as an assumption in the present study (Salomon and Lora, 2009)). This allows better assimilation of more organic loads to obtain the same removal efficiency (Barrera-Cardoso et al., 2020). Mesophilic conditions (35 + 1 °C) were considered for the current case.

The present study considered a locally previously built structure (rein- forced concrete, volume of 900 m³) corresponding to an aerobic stage of pig waste residual treatment (technological reconversion). This structure saves of approximately 80 % of the capital costs (e.g., land, building, con- struction). The energy produced is determined by the energy content of common biogas (21 MJ/m³ biogas) and the biogas potential (0.04 m³ bio- gas/kg pig manure) from the anaerobic treatment of pig manure (Hermida García, 2018).

Annually, millions of tonnes of digestate are used worldwide as fertil- izers in different crops to close the nutrient cycle in agricultural ecosystems (Bermejo et al., 2010). Digestate is considered a typical biofertilizer, owing to its composition (N, P, and K). In digestate remain several nutrients contained in feedstock that are considered essential for plant growth. Its use allows an increase in soil humus content, increasing its productivity, and a reduction in conventional synthetic fertilizers consumption. A digestate-wastewater ratio of 1:3 (% weight) is obtained from AD (Pires et al., 2012). Digestate from biodigester has a moisture content between 60 and 70 %, and it is further dried (below 50 %), reducing the deteriora- tion of organic matter (Vanderbroeck, 2021). In terms of the total- phosphorous concentration in digestate, studies in Cuba have been docu- mented ranging from 1.2 % to 2.5 % (Odales, 2017). Solar radiation for heat transfer in the open-air approach is applied. Impacts reducing coli- forms content is expected (Salihoglu et al., 2007), however, elemental com- position (C, H, o, and N) remain the same in biofertilizer (Owusu-Twum and Sharara, 2020). Precipitations impact directly the solid effluent mois- ture reduction. Annual precipitation affects the moisture change in biofertilizers. The data for annual precipitation was reported by Climate- Data (2021) (1083 mm). The water bed evaporation index is 0.33E-06 kg/h/m².

The wastewater from AD is reported for the fertirrigation of several crop plantations (Singh, 2021; Tucker, 2015). Odales (2017) concluded that ac- cording to electrical conductivity, hardness, sodium absorption relation- ship, and total soluble salts, pig wastewater in AD technology can be
attractive for soil and crops. The range of PO concentration is generally between 0.1 and 0.22 % (dry basis) (Odales, 2017).

It is assumed a conventional anaerobic-aerobic facultative lagooning treatment with COD and BOD5 removals by 64.2 o and 67.2 respectively (Sánchez-Morales et al., 2011). BOD5/COD ratio was assumed as 43.5 %, and the model used for COD composition in the lagooning effluent was proposed by Toprak (1995) and Wu and Chen (2011). Besides, a 50 % efficiency in organic matter removal is assumed in a typical pig waste lagoon (Toprak, 1995).

AD is a mature technology for managing pig waste (Zhi et al., 2021). Cuban Hybrid Technology (CHT) is a widespread technology that allows a reduction of organic matter of different diluted or concentrated residues (sugarcane, bovines, porcine, and vegetable residues). It is recognized >200 AD plants spread throughout the country, being flexible on a domes- tic scale (3-50 m³ reactor) as well as industrial scale (up to 763 m³ reactor). CHT consists of masonry rectangular technology and is covered by a rein- forced concrete structure. The digester is usually made of blocks or prefabricated structures, with vertical walls inside as a labyrinth, with a horizontal circulation geometry, similar to upflow anaerobic sludge blanket (UASB) reactor (Hermida García, 2018).

It is reported the rate of treating residues of 10 pigs/m3 reactor, is higher than fixed dome digesters (3 pigs/m r reactor). Compared with other technologies in Cuba, CHT reached a gas volumetric productivity be- tween 1 and 1.2 m³ biogas/m² digester/d (Hermida García et al., 2020), higher than other AD technologies reported in the country (e.g. fixed dome digester, floating drum digester, and inflatable tubular digester) (Hermida García, 2014). To ensure good contact between microorganisms and substrate, it is established a residual velocity limit of around 10 m/h

(Hermida García et al., 2020). Besides, it is reported an organic load re- moval of up to 60 % of COD input, and a hydraulic retention time of 2.5 d, reaching a volumetric organic load of up to 6 kg volatile solids/m³/ Biogas quality reaches 60 % methane in a continuous regime (Jiménez et al., 2015) (the rest corresponds to CO2 as an assumption in the present study (Salomon and Lora, 2009)). This allows better assimilation of more organic loads to obtain the same removal efficiency (Barrera-Cardoso et al., 2020). Mesophilic conditions (35 + 1 °C) were considered for the current case.

The present study considered a locally previously built structure (rein- forced concrete, volume of 900 m³) corresponding to an aerobic stage of pig waste residual treatment (technological reconversion). This structure saves of approximately 80 % of the capital costs (e.g., land, building, con- struction). The energy produced is determined by the energy content of common biogas (21 MJ/m³ biogas) and the biogas potential (0.04 m³ bio- gas/kg pig manure) from the anaerobic treatment of pig manure (Hermida García, 2018).

Annually, millions of tonnes of digestate are used worldwide as fertil- izers in different crops to close the nutrient cycle in agricultural ecosystems (Bermejo et al., 2010). Digestate is considered a typical biofertilizer, owing to its composition (N, P, and K). In digestate remain several nutrients contained in feedstock that are considered essential for plant growth. Its use allows an increase in soil humus content, increasing its productivity, and a reduction in conventional synthetic fertilizers consumption. A digestate-wastewater ratio of 1:3 (% weight) is obtained from AD (Pires et al., 2012). Digestate from biodigester has a moisture content between 60 and 70 %, and it is further dried (below 50 %), reducing the deteriora- tion of organic matter (Vanderbroeck, 2021). In terms of the total- phosphorous concentration in digestate, studies in Cuba have been docu- mented ranging from 1.2 % to 2.5 % (Odales, 2017). Solar radiation for heat transfer in the open-air approach is applied. Impacts reducing coli- forms content is expected (Salihoglu et al., 2007), however, elemental com- position (C, H, o, and N) remain the same in biofertilizer (Owusu-Twum and Sharara, 2020). Precipitations impact directly the solid effluent mois- ture reduction. Annual precipitation affects the moisture change in biofertilizers. The data for annual precipitation was reported by Climate- Data (2021) (1083 mm). The water bed evaporation index is 0.33E-06 kg/h/m².

The wastewater from AD is reported for the fertirrigation of several crop plantations (Singh, 2021; Tucker, 2015). Odales (2017) concluded that ac- cording to electrical conductivity, hardness, sodium absorption relation- ship, and total soluble salts, pig wastewater in AD technology can be
attractive for soil and crops. The range of PO concentration is generally between 0.1 and 0.22 % (dry basis) (Odales, 2017).

36: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) concentration in common biogas is reported in a range between 0 and 4000 ppm (Sun et al., 2015). It is widely known that H2S concentration in biogas can cause damage to internal parts of the en- ergy generation equipment, reducing its lifetime (Mulu et al., 2021). Ac- cording to the harmful impact on equipment, a purification stage is crucial for the sustainability of the technology. Two adsorption columns, using wood charcoal as an adsorbent media (marabou charcoal) are pro- posed, allowing a decrease in acquisition costs. H2S concentration in the raw biogas was considered as 2000 ppm, being 100 ppm in the biogas output stream (measured values in pig production farms with the same technology). H2S is oxidized forming SO2 when the gas is exposed to excessive oxygen, preventing S° formation inside the particle (Kanjanarong et al., 2017). The mechanism considered was suggested by Xu et al. (2014). For the energy generation scheme in the steam boiler, a dual-burner is considered inside the combustion chamber, configured as biogas/diesel as primary and secondary fuel, respectively. It was considered a diesel consump- tion index of 19.56 L/t of FW. Maximum biogas and diesel flow rates of 210 kg/h, and 100 kg/h were considered, respectively. In this way, the burner will use biogas until the pressure drop inside the pipe reaches the low re- quired pressure (3.57 kg/cm2) in 3.5 h, starting automatically diesel con- sumption. Steam from the boiler is used for FW cooking in 8 special pressured-pan technologies (six conventional pressured-pan and two thermodestructor pressured-pan), consuming up to 352 kg of diesel. Water for steam generation is not considered in the present work. A description of the alternatives considered in the current study is described below (Table 3).
37: Cuban pig feed is based mainly on soybean and corn that come from abroad. Those components carry an environmental burden according to their consumption and emissions (Gutiérrez et al., 2016). In A-2, A-3, A-4, and A-5, LF from a local supply save corn and soybean consumption by 5.72 %, 12.98 30.19 %, and 12.98 %, respectively. It was assumed that saving solid pig fodder implies fewer resources in grains cultivation, harvesting, processing, and transportation. Digestate and wastewater from AD can be considered convenient fertilizers applied to crops (Esteves al., 2019). Since pig manure is considered a valuable resource product multiple uses, digestate from composting stage and wastewa- ter from lagooning are considered avoided products to be used as biofertilizers elsewhere outside the farm. Digestate is considered an effluent with a high P content, while lower concentrations are reported for waste- water (Odales, 2017), This performance is linked to several benefits, reduc- ing fertilizer requirements to achieve the same yield, reducing the application of mineral synthetic fertilizers (Singh, 2021). The fertilizer re- placement value for P-based fertilizer was assumed to be 100 %, which is an acceptable approach suggested by according to longer time scales (Huang et al., 2011). According to Cuban regulations, digestate can substi- tute the consumption of mineral synthetic fertilizers, improving the envi- ronmental profile of the overall process. It means that every 100 kg of biofertilizer from composting stage can substitute 3.61 kg of triple super- phosphate (as diphosphorus pentoxide, P2O5), and 100 L of wastewater from lagooning can substitute 0.05 kg of the same synthetic fertilizer. Be- sides, considering crops for human feeding in the near zone of the farm (Manihot esculenta, cassava), the use of fertirrigation can avoid the use of 14,416 m³ and 28,933.2 m³ of tap water for irrigation (8.46 ha and 16.92 ha for 4000 and 8000 pigs respectively) (GOC, 2016).
38: Inventory data from pig housing were extracted from own measure- ments on-site in a TPFF, complemented with literature sources in similar pig farms (Alba-Reyes et al., 2019; Peña et al., 2013). All data were

calculated according to the functional unit (50 kg of PELU) (Appendix A). Primary data correspond to electricity, water, and fossil fuel consumption. Emissions to air, water, and soil are complemented by bibliographic sources (Hölzel et al., 2012; Jiménez et al., 2015; Stone et al., 2012). Afterward, the gathered data were validated in field visits with the farm specialist staff. Secondary data associated with pig feed (corn and soybean) are supported by data recovered from the Ecoinvent v3.6 database (Wernet et al., 2016). This includes the raw materials used for pig feed components (seeds, fertil- izers, fossil fuel, and other resources). Electricity generation was assumed, considering the current Cuban electricity breakdown (ONEI, 2019). All stages are considered as optimum performance (feed transportation, pig growing, AD, and lagooning), depending on the studied alternative.
39: <header>2.6. Life cycle impact assessment and interpretation </header>

The ReCiPe 2016 life cycle impact assessment method provides a har- monized implementation of both the midpoint and endpoint approaches (Huijbregts et al., 2016). The current study is focused on a midpoint ap- proach with a hierarchical perspective with the OpenLCA software v1.10 as an informatic tool (free open-source software) for modeling all life cycle processes (Green Delta, 2019). According to the main emissions of the overall process (CH4, CO2, NH3, COD, NO2, H2S, SO2, and BOD5), were identified six impact categories as the most used in pig production: global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity, human carcinogenic toxicity, fresh- water ecotoxicity, terrestrial acidification, and freshwater eutrophication (Alba-Reyes et al., 2019; Cherubini et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2021; McAuliffe et al., 2017).
40: A sensitivity analysis was conducted to assess the impacts of critical pa- rameter variations on the environmental profile. An evaluation of the im- pact categories in the alternative with the best performance was made to verify the strength of the results.
41: In this study, the impact of LF composition variations on LCA results was evaluated using sensitivity analysis. FW as LF impacts directly solid fodder consumption (corn and soybean). Despite FW usually being recognized with an adequate nutrient content (Chen et al., 2015), it is dynamic in com- position due to the heterogeneous sources, influencing directly the compo- sition of pig feed (García et al., 2005). Functionally, protein in the pig diet supplies essential nutrients in each animal stage, supporting a proportion- ate amount of amino acids (NRC, 2012), and it is considered critical due to its relatively expensive value in the market (Fausto-Castro et al., 2020). Besides, inadequate use of dietary protein is linked directly to nitrogen ex- cretion, causing leaching and greenhouse gas emissions in sanitary landfills (van Milgen et al., 2015). In a previous study of the personnel of TPFF, they standardize the composition of FW to achieve the nutritional requirements (ratio 1:13, tonnes of solid fodder: tonnes of LF). This feed formulation con- sidered the protein percentage as the main characteristic (50 % of the pro- tein in weight of corn and soybean) since the main objective of the farm is to obtain optimized fattened pigs. However, changes in FW composition are expected, SO these variations could alter the environmental profile of the pig production process. This analysis was addressed according to the bromatological composition of pig feed (NRC, 2012) with the main purpose to show a balanced nutritional content and that animal performance is not af- fected by these dietary variations. It was assumed that the performance of pigs was not influenced by the diet changes, since nutritional demands were completely meet. Four scenarios were compared to the best scenario (Base case) to obtain the potential impact over the selected impact categories:

<list>Base case: No variations of LF composition, all the inputs/outputs remain the same. 
S-1: A decrease in 1.01 of protein, obtaining a 1:17.53 ratio (Su et al., 2014).</list>

<list>S-2: An increase in 10.7% of protein, obtaining a 1:3.48 ratio (Meng et al., 2015). 
S-3: An increase in 0.59 % of protein, obtaining a 1:11.3 ratio (Cekmecelioglu and Uncu, 2013). 
S-4: A decrease in 0.81 % of protein, obtaining a 1:16.4 ratio (Neves et al., 2009).</list>

44: The comparison of the environmental burdens of pig production is shown in Table 4 for a 50 kg PELU as a functional unit, expressed by the six impact categories considered (characterization results).

According to the results shown in Table 4, A-1 showed worse environ- mental behavior compared with the rest of the alternatives. This perfor- mance is related to the higher solid fodder consumption for pig feeding per functional unit to guarantee the same final pig weight; A-1 consumes 18 %, 23 %, 33 %, and 23 more corn than A-2, A-3, A-4, and A-5 respec- tively. In addition, A-1 consumes 5 %, 11 %, 23 and % more soybean than the rest of the alternatives. Besides, higher feed transportation emis- sions of pollutants throughout its life cycle are obtained for this alternative. An important point to remark on is A-1 does not consider an environmen- tally friendly waste management technology. A-2 is identified as the second worse behavior in all impact categories. Despite the fossil fuel consumption is increased in A-2 compared with A-1 (115% due to the energy demand of the LF plant, the substitution of solid fodder by LF in the pig diet plays an essential role in reducing the environmental impacts. This performance supports the diversion of LF to animal feed as a sustainable approach, in- cluding waste management technologies to reduce fossil fuel consumption.

In contrast, A-4 showed the best environmental profile in all impact cat- egories. These results were attributed to a reduction in solid fodder con- sumption, as a consequence of an increase in LF production compared with the other alternatives, which lead to fewer emissions concerning con- ventional feed production (farming, transportation, milling, and storage), contributing to improving the environmental profile of this alternative. In a particular case of comparing with A-5, despite the higher diesel consump- tion and combustion-associated emissions during LF production (4.19 times), lower transportation emissions were achieved because the distance needed for KW transportation was 50 shorter compared to the distance to bring the solid fodder to the farm. Besides, a non-renewable fuel (diesel) re- placement by a renewable source (biogas) contributes to improving the en- vironmental profile of the process, obtaining a substitution of 7.96 L diesel by biogas in the LF process. Additionally, corn and soybean consumption is reduced by % indicating that the process intensity in the grain life cycle is more severe on the environmental profile than the energy use.

This result agrees with Haque et al. (2022) which obtained that the sub- stitution of grain-based pig feed with an alternative and more sustainable bioproduct (distiller-dried grain with solubles) can reduce the environmen- tal footprint by 81.4%. This behavior is associated with the reduction of N fertilizer production in grain agricultural production. A study made by Salemdeeb et al. (2017) remarked the lower environmental impacts when FW is recycled for pig feeding, suggesting that this technology is convenient for pig farms. According to the upcoming section, a discussion of the break- down activities is addressed in-depth for A-4.
3.2. Environmental analysis of activities involved in the current study

To obtain detailed information about which activity impacts the most over pig production life cycle, an assessment by activities is needed. It in- cludes pig housing (pig farming and manure management), electricity (pumping and lighting electricity consumption), feed production (corn and soybean), transportation (solid fodder and KW transportation), avoided water (wastewater for fertirrigation as a substitute for tap water for crops irrigation), and avoided fertilizer (digestate as biofertilizer, substituting mineral synthetic fertilizer). Fig. 2 shows a detailed distribution of the envi- ronmental results of the studied activities.

The activity with the best result in all impact categories was the avoided fertilizer, impacting beneficially on the six impact categories under study. This activity contributes by 78 up to 89 in all impact categories, except for global warming (43 %), terrestrial acidification (37 %), and 27 % in freshwater eutrophication, which is positioned in second place after feed production. This behavior is attributed to the process of returning nutrients to the soil with a remarkable reduction of emissions associated with min- eral synthetic fertilizers production and usage (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007). These results suggest that the replacement of mineral synthetic fer- tilizer with organic fertilizers in pig slurry treatment is a potential alterna- tive for reducing environmental impacts (Lijó et al., 2014). Avoided water impacts beneficially in all impact categories up to 1 % due to the substitu- tion of tap water used for crop irrigation. Reduction in fertilizers and broad- casters is expected when fertirrigation is implemented, supplying phosphorous to the soil and water in a nutrient dilution (Foglia et al., 2021). These results are in line with Buonocore et al. (2018) that high- lighted the use of wastewater for fertirrigation contributes to the reduction of environmental impacts, remarking that the waste treatment process lay- out must be in the near area. These findings confirm that the valorization of the digestate and the wastewater from lagooning as a substitute for mineral synthetic fertilizer, as well as the manure management implementation, are crucial to improving the environmental profile of the process.

In contrast, the activity with the worst performance is feed production, impacting harmfully in all studied activities. It contributes from % to (depending on the impact category), being freshwater eutrophication (71 %), terrestrial acidification (62 %), and global warming (50 the greatest contributors. Freshwater eutrophication was the most significant due to the potential of P and nitrate leaching to the surface and groundwa- ter. The majority of the global warming from corn and soybean corresponds to field emissions and deforestation. Besides, field emission from corn mainly contributes to the impacts of terrestrial acidification. Authors like Noya et al. (2017) evaluated two concentrated feed production in Spain and reported feed production activity was the highest contributor (from 14% to 78 depending on the impact categories) in climate change, fresh- water eutrophication, and marine eutrophication mainly driven by the con- tribution of ingredients production (mainly corn and soybean). This research considered a similar system boundary compared to the current study, remarking on the potential improvements given by local ingredients to supply conventional solid fodder. Besides, Groen et al. (2016) demon- strated that feed production accounted for up to 67 of the total pig pro- duction profile due to their emissions during its life cycle, mainly GHG (nitrous oxides and carbon dioxide). A rational conclusion could be that pig feed will be responsible for most of the environmental burdens in pig pro- duction. However, Zheng et al. (2021) demonstrated that depending on the system boundaries understudy, activities such as pig production could impact
Environmental profile of one PELU (50 kg) as a functional unit.

more harmfully over impact categories like terrestrial acidification, eutrophi- cation potential, and eutrophication terrestrial. Aspects such as modeling as- sumptions and input/output parameters can add uncertainties to the results.

Pig housing is the activity with the second worse behavior, impacting harmfully in all studies activities. The contributions are from 1 % up to 8 %. Pig enteric fermentation is influenced by CH4, NH3, and CO2 emis- sions, meaning that physiological processes attributable to the pig growing stages are minor contributors to the total life cycle. Electricity and transpor- tation activities made negligible contributions to the impacts on the global environmental profile. This behavior is attributed to lower consumption of diesel and electricity in the life cycle. Despite the increase of diesel con- sumption in LF production per functional unit, the substitution of fossil fuel for biogas plays an important role in reducing global impacts. In general, feed production is identified as the activity with the worst environmental performance according to the impact categories under study. Consequently, cleaner production should be directed to reduce the impacts of solid fodder on typical Cuban pig production farms. Besides, AD as a waste management approach can be a sustainable alternative, not only for producing biogas and substituting fossil fuel but also to produce an easy-handle biofertilizer, being an environmental potential when min- eral synthetic fertilizers are substituted.
45: The comparison of the environmental burdens of pig production is shown in Table 4 for a 50 kg PELU as a functional unit, expressed by the six impact categories considered (characterization results).

According to the results shown in Table 4, A-1 showed worse environ- mental behavior compared with the rest of the alternatives. This perfor- mance is related to the higher solid fodder consumption for pig feeding per functional unit to guarantee the same final pig weight; A-1 consumes 18 %, 23 %, 33 %, and 23 more corn than A-2, A-3, A-4, and A-5 respec- tively. In addition, A-1 consumes 5 %, 11 %, 23 and % more soybean than the rest of the alternatives. Besides, higher feed transportation emis- sions of pollutants throughout its life cycle are obtained for this alternative. An important point to remark on is A-1 does not consider an environmen- tally friendly waste management technology. A-2 is identified as the second worse behavior in all impact categories. Despite the fossil fuel consumption is increased in A-2 compared with A-1 (115% due to the energy demand of the LF plant, the substitution of solid fodder by LF in the pig diet plays an essential role in reducing the environmental impacts. This performance supports the diversion of LF to animal feed as a sustainable approach, in- cluding waste management technologies to reduce fossil fuel consumption.

In contrast, A-4 showed the best environmental profile in all impact cat- egories. These results were attributed to a reduction in solid fodder con- sumption, as a consequence of an increase in LF production compared with the other alternatives, which lead to fewer emissions concerning con- ventional feed production (farming, transportation, milling, and storage), contributing to improving the environmental profile of this alternative. In a particular case of comparing with A-5, despite the higher diesel consump- tion and combustion-associated emissions during LF production (4.19 times), lower transportation emissions were achieved because the distance needed for KW transportation was 50 shorter compared to the distance to bring the solid fodder to the farm. Besides, a non-renewable fuel (diesel) re- placement by a renewable source (biogas) contributes to improving the en- vironmental profile of the process, obtaining a substitution of 7.96 L diesel by biogas in the LF process. Additionally, corn and soybean consumption is reduced by % indicating that the process intensity in the grain life cycle is more severe on the environmental profile than the energy use.

This result agrees with Haque et al. (2022) which obtained that the sub- stitution of grain-based pig feed with an alternative and more sustainable bioproduct (distiller-dried grain with solubles) can reduce the environmen- tal footprint by 81.4%. This behavior is associated with the reduction of N fertilizer production in grain agricultural production. A study made by Salemdeeb et al. (2017) remarked the lower environmental impacts when FW is recycled for pig feeding, suggesting that this technology is convenient for pig farms. According to the upcoming section, a discussion of the break- down activities is addressed in-depth for A-4.
3.2. Environmental analysis of activities involved in the current study

To obtain detailed information about which activity impacts the most over pig production life cycle, an assessment by activities is needed. It in- cludes pig housing (pig farming and manure management), electricity (pumping and lighting electricity consumption), feed production (corn and soybean), transportation (solid fodder and KW transportation), avoided water (wastewater for fertirrigation as a substitute for tap water for crops irrigation), and avoided fertilizer (digestate as biofertilizer, substituting mineral synthetic fertilizer). Fig. 2 shows a detailed distribution of the envi- ronmental results of the studied activities.

The activity with the best result in all impact categories was the avoided fertilizer, impacting beneficially on the six impact categories under study. This activity contributes by 78 up to 89 in all impact categories, except for global warming (43 %), terrestrial acidification (37 %), and 27 % in freshwater eutrophication, which is positioned in second place after feed production. This behavior is attributed to the process of returning nutrients to the soil with a remarkable reduction of emissions associated with min- eral synthetic fertilizers production and usage (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007). These results suggest that the replacement of mineral synthetic fer- tilizer with organic fertilizers in pig slurry treatment is a potential alterna- tive for reducing environmental impacts (Lijó et al., 2014). Avoided water impacts beneficially in all impact categories up to 1 % due to the substitu- tion of tap water used for crop irrigation. Reduction in fertilizers and broad- casters is expected when fertirrigation is implemented, supplying phosphorous to the soil and water in a nutrient dilution (Foglia et al., 2021). These results are in line with Buonocore et al. (2018) that high- lighted the use of wastewater for fertirrigation contributes to the reduction of environmental impacts, remarking that the waste treatment process lay- out must be in the near area. These findings confirm that the valorization of the digestate and the wastewater from lagooning as a substitute for mineral synthetic fertilizer, as well as the manure management implementation, are crucial to improving the environmental profile of the process.

In contrast, the activity with the worst performance is feed production, impacting harmfully in all studied activities. Environmental profile of one PELU (50 kg) as a functional unit.
