This behavior is associated with the reduction of N fertilizer production in grain agricultural production. A study made by Salemdeeb et al. (2017) remarked the lower environmental impacts when FW is recycled for pig feeding, suggesting that this technology is convenient for pig farms. According to the upcoming section, a discussion of the break- down activities is addressed in-depth for A-4.
3.2. Environmental analysis of activities involved in the current study

To obtain detailed information about which activity impacts the most over pig production life cycle, an assessment by activities is needed. It in- cludes pig housing (pig farming and manure management), electricity (pumping and lighting electricity consumption), feed production (corn and soybean), transportation (solid fodder and KW transportation), avoided water (wastewater for fertirrigation as a substitute for tap water for crops irrigation), and avoided fertilizer (digestate as biofertilizer, substituting mineral synthetic fertilizer). Fig. 2 shows a detailed distribution of the envi- ronmental results of the studied activities.

The activity with the best result in all impact categories was the avoided fertilizer, impacting beneficially on the six impact categories under study. This activity contributes by 78 up to 89 in all impact categories, except for global warming (43 %), terrestrial acidification (37 %), and 27 % in freshwater eutrophication, which is positioned in second place after feed production. This behavior is attributed to the process of returning nutrients to the soil with a remarkable reduction of emissions associated with min- eral synthetic fertilizers production and usage (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007). These results suggest that the replacement of mineral synthetic fer- tilizer with organic fertilizers in pig slurry treatment is a potential alterna- tive for reducing environmental impacts (Lijó et al., 2014). Avoided water impacts beneficially in all impact categories up to 1 % due to the substitu- tion of tap water used for crop irrigation. Reduction in fertilizers and broad- casters is expected when fertirrigation is implemented, supplying phosphorous to the soil and water in a nutrient dilution (Foglia et al., 2021). These results are in line with Buonocore et al. (2018) that high- lighted the use of wastewater for fertirrigation contributes to the reduction of environmental impacts, remarking that the waste treatment process lay- out must be in the near area. These findings confirm that the valorization of the digestate and the wastewater from lagooning as a substitute for mineral synthetic fertilizer, as well as the manure management implementation, are crucial to improving the environmental profile of the process.

In contrast, the activity with the worst performance is feed production, impacting harmfully in all studied activities. It contributes from % to (depending on the impact category), being freshwater eutrophication (71 %), terrestrial acidification (62 %), and global warming (50 the greatest contributors. Freshwater eutrophication was the most significant due to the potential of P and nitrate leaching to the surface and groundwa- ter. The majority of the global warming from corn and soybean corresponds to field emissions and deforestation. Besides, field emission from corn mainly contributes to the impacts of terrestrial acidification. Authors like Noya et al. (2017) evaluated two concentrated feed production in Spain and reported feed production activity was the highest contributor (from 14% to 78 depending on the impact categories) in climate change, fresh- water eutrophication, and marine eutrophication mainly driven by the con- tribution of ingredients production (mainly corn and soybean). This research considered a similar system boundary compared to the current study, remarking on the potential improvements given by local ingredients to supply conventional solid fodder. Besides, Groen et al. (2016) demon- strated that feed production accounted for up to 67 of the total pig pro- duction profile due to their emissions during its life cycle, mainly GHG (nitrous oxides and carbon dioxide). A rational conclusion could be that pig feed will be responsible for most of the environmental burdens in pig pro- duction. However, Zheng et al. (2021) demonstrated that depending on the system boundaries understudy, activities such as pig production could impact
Environmental profile of one PELU (50 kg) as a functional unit.

more harmfully over impact categories like terrestrial acidification, eutrophi- cation potential, and eutrophication terrestrial. Aspects such as modeling as- sumptions and input/output parameters can add uncertainties to the results.

Pig housing is the activity with the second worse behavior, impacting harmfully in all studies activities. The contributions are from 1 % up to 8 %. Pig enteric fermentation is influenced by CH4, NH3, and CO2 emis- sions, meaning that physiological processes attributable to the pig growing stages are minor contributors to the total life cycle. Electricity and transpor- tation activities made negligible contributions to the impacts on the global environmental profile. This behavior is attributed to lower consumption of diesel and electricity in the life cycle. Despite the increase of diesel con- sumption in LF production per functional unit, the substitution of fossil fuel for biogas plays an important role in reducing global impacts. In general, feed production is identified as the activity with the worst environmental performance according to the impact categories under study. Consequently, cleaner production should be directed to reduce the impacts of solid fodder on typical Cuban pig production farms. Besides, AD as a waste management approach can be a sustainable alternative, not only for producing biogas and substituting fossil fuel but also to produce an easy-handle biofertilizer, being an environmental potential when min- eral synthetic fertilizers are substituted.
49: The comparison of the environmental burdens of pig production is shown in Table 4 for a 50 kg PELU as a functional unit, expressed by the six impact categories considered (characterization results).

According to the results shown in Table 4, A-1 showed worse environ- mental behavior compared with the rest of the alternatives. This perfor- mance is related to the higher solid fodder consumption for pig feeding per functional unit to guarantee the same final pig weight; A-1 consumes 18 %, 23 %, 33 %, and 23 more corn than A-2, A-3, A-4, and A-5 respec- tively. In addition, A-1 consumes 5 %, 11 %, 23 and % more soybean than the rest of the alternatives. Besides, higher feed transportation emis- sions of pollutants throughout its life cycle are obtained for this alternative. An important point to remark on is A-1 does not consider an environmen- tally friendly waste management technology. A-2 is identified as the second worse behavior in all impact categories. Despite the fossil fuel consumption is increased in A-2 compared with A-1 (115% due to the energy demand of the LF plant, the substitution of solid fodder by LF in the pig diet plays an essential role in reducing the environmental impacts. This performance supports the diversion of LF to animal feed as a sustainable approach, in- cluding waste management technologies to reduce fossil fuel consumption.

In contrast, A-4 showed the best environmental profile in all impact cat- egories. These results were attributed to a reduction in solid fodder con- sumption, as a consequence of an increase in LF production compared with the other alternatives, which lead to fewer emissions concerning con- ventional feed production (farming, transportation, milling, and storage), contributing to improving the environmental profile of this alternative. In a particular case of comparing with A-5, despite the higher diesel consump- tion and combustion-associated emissions during LF production (4.19 times), lower transportation emissions were achieved because the distance needed for KW transportation was 50 shorter compared to the distance to bring the solid fodder to the farm. Besides, a non-renewable fuel (diesel) re- placement by a renewable source (biogas) contributes to improving the en- vironmental profile of the process, obtaining a substitution of 7.96 L diesel by biogas in the LF process. Additionally, corn and soybean consumption is reduced by % indicating that the process intensity in the grain life cycle is more severe on the environmental profile than the energy use.

This result agrees with Haque et al. (2022) which obtained that the sub- stitution of grain-based pig feed with an alternative and more sustainable bioproduct (distiller-dried grain with solubles) can reduce the environmen- tal footprint by 81.4%. This behavior is associated with the reduction of N fertilizer production in grain agricultural production. A study made by Salemdeeb et al. (2017) remarked the lower environmental impacts when FW is recycled for pig feeding, suggesting that this technology is convenient for pig farms. According to the upcoming section, a discussion of the break- down activities is addressed in-depth for A-4.

According to the results shown in Table 4, A-1 showed worse environ- mental behavior compared with the rest of the alternatives. This perfor- mance is related to the higher solid fodder consumption for pig feeding per functional unit to guarantee the same final pig weight; A-1 consumes 18 %, 23 %, 33 %, and 23 more corn than A-2, A-3, A-4, and A-5 respec- tively. In addition, A-1 consumes 5 %, 11 %, 23 and % more soybean than the rest of the alternatives. Besides, higher feed transportation emis- sions of pollutants throughout its life cycle are obtained for this alternative. An important point to remark on is A-1 does not consider an environmen- tally friendly waste management technology. A-2 is identified as the second worse behavior in all impact categories. Despite the fossil fuel consumption is increased in A-2 compared with A-1 (115% due to the energy demand of the LF plant, the substitution of solid fodder by LF in the pig diet plays an essential role in reducing the environmental impacts. This performance supports the diversion of LF to animal feed as a sustainable approach, in- cluding waste management technologies to reduce fossil fuel consumption.

In contrast, A-4 showed the best environmental profile in all impact cat- egories. These results were attributed to a reduction in solid fodder con- sumption, as a consequence of an increase in LF production compared with the other alternatives, which lead to fewer emissions concerning con- ventional feed production (farming, transportation, milling, and storage), contributing to improving the environmental profile of this alternative. In a particular case of comparing with A-5, despite the higher diesel consump- tion and combustion-associated emissions during LF production (4.19 times), lower transportation emissions were achieved because the distance needed for KW transportation was 50 shorter compared to the distance to bring the solid fodder to the farm. Besides, a non-renewable fuel (diesel) re- placement by a renewable source (biogas) contributes to improving the en- vironmental profile of the process, obtaining a substitution of 7.96 L diesel by biogas in the LF process. Additionally, corn and soybean consumption is reduced by % indicating that the process intensity in the grain life cycle is more severe on the environmental profile than the energy use.

This result agrees with Haque et al. (2022) which obtained that the sub- stitution of grain-based pig feed with an alternative and more sustainable bioproduct (distiller-dried grain with solubles) can reduce the environmen- tal footprint by 81.4%. This behavior is associated with the reduction of N fertilizer production in grain agricultural production. A study made by Salemdeeb et al. (2017) remarked the lower environmental impacts when FW is recycled for pig feeding, suggesting that this technology is convenient for pig farms. According to the upcoming section, a discussion of the break- down activities is addressed in-depth for A-4.
3.2. Environmental analysis of activities involved in the current study

To obtain detailed information about which activity impacts the most over pig production life cycle, an assessment by activities is needed. It in- cludes pig housing (pig farming and manure management), electricity (pumping and lighting electricity consumption), feed production (corn and soybean), transportation (solid fodder and KW transportation), avoided water (wastewater for fertirrigation as a substitute for tap water for crops irrigation), and avoided fertilizer (digestate as biofertilizer, substituting mineral synthetic fertilizer). Fig. 2 shows a detailed distribution of the envi- ronmental results of the studied activities.

The activity with the best result in all impact categories was the avoided fertilizer, impacting beneficially on the six impact categories under study. This activity contributes by 78 up to 89 in all impact categories, except for global warming (43 %), terrestrial acidification (37 %), and 27 % in freshwater eutrophication, which is positioned in second place after feed production. This behavior is attributed to the process of returning nutrients to the soil with a remarkable reduction of emissions associated with min- eral synthetic fertilizers production and usage (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007). These results suggest that the replacement of mineral synthetic fer- tilizer with organic fertilizers in pig slurry treatment is a potential alterna- tive for reducing environmental impacts (Lijó et al., 2014). Avoided water impacts beneficially in all impact categories up to 1 % due to the substitu- tion of tap water used for crop irrigation. Reduction in fertilizers and broad- casters is expected when fertirrigation is implemented, supplying phosphorous to the soil and water in a nutrient dilution (Foglia et al., 2021). These results are in line with Buonocore et al. (2018) that high- lighted the use of wastewater for fertirrigation contributes to the reduction of environmental impacts, remarking that the waste treatment process lay- out must be in the near area. These findings confirm that the valorization of the digestate and the wastewater from lagooning as a substitute for mineral synthetic fertilizer, as well as the manure management implementation, are crucial to improving the environmental profile of the process.

In contrast, the activity with the worst performance is feed production, impacting harmfully in all studied activities. It contributes from % to (depending on the impact category), being freshwater eutrophication (71 %), terrestrial acidification (62 %), and global warming (50 the greatest contributors. Freshwater eutrophication was the most significant due to the potential of P and nitrate leaching to the surface and groundwa- ter. The majority of the global warming from corn and soybean corresponds to field emissions and deforestation. Besides, field emission from corn mainly contributes to the impacts of terrestrial acidification. Authors like Noya et al. (2017) evaluated two concentrated feed production in Spain and reported feed production activity was the highest contributor (from 14% to 78 depending on the impact categories) in climate change, fresh- water eutrophication, and marine eutrophication mainly driven by the con- tribution of ingredients production (mainly corn and soybean). This research considered a similar system boundary compared to the current study, remarking on the potential improvements given by local ingredients to supply conventional solid fodder. Besides, Groen et al. (2016) demon- strated that feed production accounted for up to 67 of the total pig pro- duction profile due to their emissions during its life cycle, mainly GHG (nitrous oxides and carbon dioxide). A rational conclusion could be that pig feed will be responsible for most of the environmental burdens in pig pro- duction. However, Zheng et al. (2021) demonstrated that depending on the system boundaries understudy, activities such as pig production could impact
Environmental profile of one PELU (50 kg) as a functional unit.

more harmfully over impact categories like terrestrial acidification, eutrophi- cation potential, and eutrophication terrestrial. Aspects such as modeling as- sumptions and input/output parameters can add uncertainties to the results.

Pig housing is the activity with the second worse behavior, impacting harmfully in all studies activities. The contributions are from 1 % up to 8 %. Pig enteric fermentation is influenced by CH4, NH3, and CO2 emis- sions, meaning that physiological processes attributable to the pig growing stages are minor contributors to the total life cycle. Electricity and transpor- tation activities made negligible contributions to the impacts on the global environmental profile. This behavior is attributed to lower consumption of diesel and electricity in the life cycle. Despite the increase of diesel con- sumption in LF production per functional unit, the substitution of fossil fuel for biogas plays an important role in reducing global impacts. In general, feed production is identified as the activity with the worst environmental performance according to the impact categories under study. Consequently, cleaner production should be directed to reduce the impacts of solid fodder on typical Cuban pig production farms. Besides, AD as a waste management approach can be a sustainable alternative, not only for producing biogas and substituting fossil fuel but also to produce an easy-handle biofertilizer, being an environmental potential when min- eral synthetic fertilizers are substituted.

54: <header>3.3. Breakdown by feed production and avoided product activities </header>

Fig. 3 depicts the environmental profile of feed production and avoided products (avoided water and avoided fertilizer) activities. In the case of solid fodder production, it involves several operations such as machine op- eration (soil cultivation, sowing, fertilization, pesticide application, har- vesting, and on-farm transport), fuel consumption, and sheds (Green Delta, 2019). As previously identified, corn and soybean are the most used solid fodder on Cuban farms. The impact on the life cycle of corn is
Fig. 3. Distribution burdens (%) of the avoided product activities (avoided water, avoided fertilizer) and avoided feed (corn and soybean) with the major impacts in A-4.

in the range of 52 % up to 95 % depending on the impact category. The cause of this performance is related to intensive corn seed production, nitro- gen ecoprofile, and post-harvesting treatments (corn drying) than soybean (Haque and Liu, 2019). This suggests that despite pig feeding components being necessary to obtain pigs with the optimum weight on the established period, a substitution of a convenient percentage of corn by alternatives and locally available sources of proteins such as tithonia diversifolia, moringa oleifera, morus alba, and hibiscus rosa-sinensis should be considered to reduce environmental impacts of the pig production in Cuba (Savón et al., 2008; Valdivié-Navarro et al., 2020). However, further studies are necessary to es- tablish its role in the pig production life cycle.

The substitution of a mineral synthetic fertilizer with fertilizer from a natural source contributes to reducing emissions (Ioannou-Ttofa et al., 2021). Those emissions are associated with organic compounds and heavy metals related to the fertilizer industrial process flow during its life cycle. An important point to bear in mind is the high P concentration in biofertilizer and wastewater, allowing the substitution of a higher amount of triple superphosphate per functional unit. In all impact categories, avoided fertilizer production implies a beneficial impact of >99 %, associ- ated with harmful impacts over water eutrophication, acidification, and particulate matter formation (Renard et al., 2004). Besides, a decrease in N2O emissions impacts beneficially, as it is considered one of the main pre- cursors of climate change, as well as reducing emissions to the water of NO3 and PO (leaching) which can cause eutrophication. Regarding P leaching, it is reported that despite most of P in digestate being found as re- calcitrant compounds, its runoff opportunity is lower compared with min- eral synthetic fertilizers (Lu et al., 2012). In addition, heavy metals emissions to soil (ecotoxicity) avoid the production of P-based mineral syn- thetic fertilizers, contributing to the reduction of environmental burdens (Brockmann et al., 2014). In terms of the potential impacts of terrestrial acidification, the beneficial impacts are associated with the reduction of sulfuric oxides emissions corresponding to triple superphosphate manufacturing (Silva and Kulay, 2003). Moreover, applications of digestate as a biofertilizer to soil save GHG emissions compared with crude manure application since the AD process reduce organic matter and nitrogen spe- cies volatilization (Esteves et al., 2019). Even though additional research is necessary to study the potential of biofertilizer as a substitute for mineral synthetic fertilizer applied to local crops, these preliminary results suggest the potential reduction in the environmental profile when pig waste is man- aged using AD technology.

According to avoided products, avoided water used in fertirrigation pre- sented lower impacts than avoided fertilizer (below %) on the six impact categories under study. This result is associated with lower resource con- sumption and pollutants emitted during this activity compared to avoided fertilizer activity. However, further studies should be made since wastewa- ter reported three times more nitrogen concentration than digestate (Brockmann et al., 2014). In addition, it can have a beneficial impact on the water footprint of the overall process, in addition to replacing irrigation

water, supplying a considerable amount of mineral fertilizer could be ob- tained in irrigation activities covering part of the crops nutrient demand (Diaz-Elsayed et al., 2019). Since irrigation water is obtained by recycling reclaimed water, beneficial impacts are expected in the water depletion im- pact category. However, harmful impacts are expected in human toxicity and terrestrial ecotoxicity due to the addition of heavy metals to the water (Foglia et al., 2021), an aspect that can be assessed in additional studies.

Moreover, applications of digestate as a biofertilizer to soil save GHG emissions compared with crude manure application since the AD process reduce organic matter and nitrogen spe- cies volatilization (Esteves et al., 2019). Even though additional research is necessary to study the potential of biofertilizer as a substitute for mineral synthetic fertilizer applied to local crops, these preliminary results suggest the potential reduction in the environmental profile when pig waste is man- aged using AD technology.

According to avoided products, avoided water used in fertirrigation pre- sented lower impacts than avoided fertilizer (below %) on the six impact categories under study. This result is associated with lower resource con- sumption and pollutants emitted during this activity compared to avoided fertilizer activity. However, further studies should be made since wastewa- ter reported three times more nitrogen concentration than digestate (Brockmann et al., 2014). In addition, it can have a beneficial impact on the water footprint of the overall process, in addition to replacing irrigation

water, supplying a considerable amount of mineral fertilizer could be ob- tained in irrigation activities covering part of the crops nutrient demand (Diaz-Elsayed et al., 2019). Since irrigation water is obtained by recycling reclaimed water, beneficial impacts are expected in the water depletion im- pact category. However, harmful impacts are expected in human toxicity and terrestrial ecotoxicity due to the addition of heavy metals to the water (Foglia et al., 2021), an aspect that can be assessed in additional studies.
58: The current subsection describes the environmental performance of the impact categories previously identified: global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity, human carcinogenic toxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, terrestrial acidification, and freshwater eutrophication.
59: Results of global warming are expressed in kg CO2-eq, and it is corre- lated with non-renewable energy demand. Based on the results, the largest hotspot emissions are associated with feed production, especially corn production (26.5 of the total impact, followed by soybean production (16.5 Corn and soybean come from abroad (the United States and Brazil respectively), and they are associated with deforestation (Ottosen et al., 2021). Similar results are reported by Alba-Reyes et al. (2019) (Cuba), remarking on the high impacts of feed production on the global en- vironmental profile. Besides, a pig diet highly dependent on corn is one of the main causes, despite it covers up to 48 % in weight (soybean corre- sponds to the rest); it is referred to as a high-intensive crop considering fer- tilizing issues compared with other solid fodder components (McAuliffe et al., 2017). CO2 emissions related to land transformation in corn produc- tion are responsible for 71 % of the total process. Meanwhile, transporta- tion plays an important role according to the emissions of CO2 and CH4 mainly. In the current activity, it is essential to remark that the relationship between solid fodder and KW is 201:1 in weight; this means that it is neces- sary 200 times more corn and soybean than KW approximately; adding that the distance between the pig farm and the port is double. For that reason, in all the emissions, the transportation of solid fodder represents >99% of the total. This statement agrees with Hamelin et al. (2014) that remarked on the critical role of transportation in the environmental profile of biogas plants. In the case of soybean production, N20 corresponds to 55.5 of the total emissions. Mineral synthetic fertilizer substitution impacts benefi- cially on global warming, accounting for up to 43 %. The same beneficial impact was shown by Ramirez-Islas et al. (2020) and Brockmann et al. (2014) when the impacts on climate change are reduced by 0.05 kg CO2 eq and by 20 % in global emissions. The same results were obtained by Prapaspongsa et al. (2010), reducing up to 147 kg CO2-eq per tonne of pig manure using AD as a waste treatment technology. The current alterna- tive showed the potential impacts that could be avoided by applying cleaner production such as AD, impacting beneficially the environmental profile.
60: Terrestrial ecotoxicity is expressed in kg of 1.4-dichlorobenzene-equiv- alents (1.4-DCB-eq) and it is attributed to heavy metal emission (Huijbregts et al., 2016). Corn and soybean are the most relevant process that affects terrestrial ecotoxicity. This performance is associated with the production and application of pesticides during the crop's life cycle. The substances that impacted the most over the current impact category are the heavy metals (emissions to air) such as copper (60 and 50 for corn and soy- bean production, respectively) and vanadium (15 and 14% for corn and soybean production, respectively). According to the previous discussion, solid fodder production is considered a crucial stage; mainly for the addi- tives and emissions associated with its life cycle, however, the substitution of solid fodder with more environmentally pig feeding components can in- fluence beneficially the environmental profile of the pig production
scheme. These findings are consistent with Stone et al. (2012) which reported detrimental impacts of a corn-soymeal-based feed of up to
61: The results (fate and effects of chemical substances) are expressed in kg of 1.4-DCB-eq. In a global approach, the current impact category performed beneficially, determined by the substitution of mineral synthetic fertilizer. In particular, avoiding the release of heavy metals such as chromium VI into the groundwater and nickel into the groundwater and the air repre- sents 67 % and 4.3% respectively, contributing the most to human carci- nogenic toxicity. Those impacts occurred during the industrial process on the fertilizer life cycle. Feed production, especially corn, contributes the most to human carcinogenic toxicity about 11.49 of the total. Pig housing is related to 7.9 %, associated with on-farm enteric emissions. The rest of the activities contribute negligibly. In a closer look at the substance that impacts harmfully over this impact category, chromium VI represents up to 53 %.
62: Feed production is identified as the activity with the worst impact (12.47 % associated with the release of metals like copper, zinc, and sil- ver. The current results are lower than the one proposed by González- García et al. (2015) that affirmed that crop and pig feed production contrib- utes 99 % of the freshwater ecotoxicity impact category. Lower values are reported by Liu et al. (2021) (82 % dominated by corn production at 48 % compared with other feed components like wheat grain, barley, soybean meal, extruded soybean, and others. Zinc and copper are the substances that impacted the most, with values up to 75 % and 21 %, respectively. The beneficial impact of this impact category regarding the substitution of mineral synthetic P-based fertilizer contributes to a reduction of environ- mental burdens (environmental credits) of 84.25% mainly by avoiding the impacts associated with the use of chemical raw materials and fossil fuels.
63: This impact category studies the change in the acidity in soil due to the emissions of substances such as sulfates, phosphates, and nitrates. Corn pro- duction derived a harmful contribution of up to 60 % of the total process, followed by a beneficial contribution of 38 % of the avoiding of mineral synthetic fertilizer production. Despite digestate is recognized as an appro- priate substitute for synthetic mineral synthetic fertilizer (Styles et al., 2016; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015); Styles et al., 2016), attention needs to be paid to over-fertilization in crop fields (Van Stappen et al., 2016; Zeng et al., 2016). To reduce this behavior, fertilization using biofertilizer has to be made according to a sustainable approach, considering the composi- tion of the soil. The current work agrees with McAuliffe et al. (2017) who affirmed feed production impacts between 45 % to 66 % over the total im- pact. However, authors like Nguyen et al. (2011) and Reckmann et al. (2013) reported impacts on feed production of 36 % and 23 respectively, due to the consideration of a more complex grain-based diet. The main sub- stances that contribute the most to terrestrial acidification are NH3 (95% NO2 (1.4%) and SO2 (1.1 %).
64: In general, the substitution of mineral synthetic fertilizer represents a beneficial impact of up to 60 %, mainly to avoid phosphate emissions to aquatic bodies. Feed-related products were the highest harmful contributor to freshwater eutrophication, accounting for up to 32 % (17 related to corn production, and 15 % to soybean). P and phosphate emissions (33% and 25 respectively) are the main precursors of eutrophication potential in crop production. These results are higher compared with Alba-Reyes et al. (2019), which reported a contribution of 7.91 over this impact cat- egory, mainly by the consideration of pig housing activity along with waste management technology (lagooning). The difference in the contribution is due to the consideration of a higher amount of avoided product in the use of digestate as biofertilizer and wastewater for fertirrigation. Environmental
burdens associated with pig housing represent up to 4 %. The rest of the ac- tivities contribute negligibly.
65: As per previous results, A-4 has performed the best environmental alter- native. For that reason, it was found that the change in the LF composition impacts all impact categories (Fig. 4). As expected, the environmental pro- file with the best-case scenario performance is S-2, since the consumption of corn and soybean was reduced by %, and 83% respectively compared with the Base case (A-4), with emissions of 4.26 kg CO2-eq, indicating that when the protein composition in LF increases, the amount of solid fodder is reduced. Besides, the process involved to obtain those crops (corn and soy- bean), impacted significantly the environmental profile, reducing impacts. These results brought up a decrease compared with the original alternative (Base case) in 95 % of the total CO2-eq. This behavior is explained by less solid fodder (corn and soybean) consumption and fewer emissions in diesel combustion, suggesting that change in protein composition has a very sen- sitive effect on the impact categories under assessment. Although this sce- nario is theoretical, it highlights the potential environmental changes that could be achieved when more solid fodder is substituted by LF.

As can be seen in Fig. 4, the worst-case scenario is S-1 in all impact cat- legories studied (global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity, human carcino- genic toxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, terrestrial acidification, and freshwater eutrophication). This performance is associated with an increase in solid fodder consumption compared with the Base case. These results suggest that the consideration of alternative components for pig feeding can reduce substantially the potential impact on the environment. The re- sults of the present study are in line with the reported literature, indicating that when grain components in the pig diet are substituted, the environ- mental impacts are reduced (acidification, eutrophication, and global warming potential), mainly when those components correspond to local production (Wilfart et al., 2016).

The impact category with the highest sensitivity is global warming, where the changes compared with the Base case are noticeable, above 26 % in S-1, and below 95 % in S-2. This suggests that the emission of GHG, such as CO2 and N20, are important drivers to change the environ- mental impact and need more attention. In contrast, the impact category with the lowest sensitivity is terrestrial ecotoxicity. The variations are up

to 2 % in S-1, and below 8 % in S-2, demonstrating lesser impacts of heavy metals over the pig production process. Eriksson et al. (2005) and van Zanten et al. (2014) reported significant climate change reduction im- pacts when soybean is reduced by alternative pig feed components. Besides, De Quelen et al. (2021) assessed the impacts of alternative protein sources locally produced feed components, reducing cereals and oils meals in pig diets. The results showed lower environmental impacts on climate change compared with the control diet in all pig growing stages due to the high impact of soybean meal, associated with deforestation in the origin harvesting place.

Based on these results, supporting non-conventional feeding ingredients of pig production and using AD as waste management technology have ad- vantages from an environmental perspective. However, it is necessary to follow good practices in waste management technologies to reduce the im- pacts of different effluents, reducing organic matter and GHG emissions. This is a clear goal for developing countries to make efficient use of renew- able energy technologies (AD), mitigating the potential impacts on the en- vironment.

68: <header>3.6. Observations about assumptions in the current study </header>

Despite the common use of local data in the current inventory (fore- ground data), some uncertainties are related to KW composition. Generally, diets in pig production are mostly formulated for economic profitability, so, nutritional specifications are often following this conception (Mackenzie et al., 2016). In this sense, assumptions about standard composition are needed. In terms of transportation, higher KW consumption implies higher fossil fuel consumption, emitting greater amounts of polluting gases. Be- sides, more fuel is required when more trips are necessary (longer distances to the port in this case), related to higher solid fodder consumption. This means that according to the distance and the number of trips, global warming is the more potentially impacted category (Ogino et al., 2007). Fossil fuel consumption (diesel) in LF production can show a similar pattern according to GHG emissions, impacting the most over global warming, in this case, the higher amount of NO2. Besides, transportation impacts vary significantly according to traffic, road conditions, and the number of stops (Bacenetti et al., 2013). In addition, environmental credits for FW di- verting to LF were not considered in the current treatment routes, which can stimulate companies to reduce their carbon emissions.
Fig. 4. Sensitivity analysis in four scenarios (S-1, S-2, S-3, and S-4) compared with Base case (A-4).

The reduction of solid fodder by using KW as a substitute impact all im- pact categories, while corn and soybean are considered principal drivers in al- most all impact categories considered in the current study. The exclusion of infrastructure and maintenance of pig production, as well as the slaughtering process, could carry uncertainty in environmental burdens. Moreover, the generation of electricity using biogas, and the consideration of other waste management alternatives (e.g. incineration, and composting), could bring a different approach to the current scenario.

Regarding the allocation approach, in the case of water irrigation, it was considered a water technology for a selected crop in the nearby area (Manihot esculenta, cassava). However, if it is considered other common crops like Saccharum officinarum, Pennisetun purpureun, Musa paradisiaca, and Ipomoea batatas, the water irrigation standard can be reduced by 59 %, 56 %, 74.47 and 81.88 respectively. This approach will impact directly terrestrial ecotoxicity 1.4-DCB), human carcinogenic toxicity - 3.93 kg 1.4-DCB), and freshwater eutrophication 0.04 kg P- eq). Besides, the potential impact could be found in substituting other typ- ical synthetic fertilizers like ammonium phosphate, and potassium chlo- ride. In a study made by Alba-Reyes et al. (2019), a beneficial impact is expected in all impact categories, mainly in climate change and terrestrial acidification.

Since the soil application of digestate as biofertilizer is not within the system boundaries of the present study, the positive effects are not evalu- ated (considering the soil improvement). However, heavy metal emissions, as well as pathogens and antibiotics, could impact harmfully the environ- mental profile of the process, mainly on human toxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, and terrestrial ecotoxicity. To date, there is research lack on this direction.

This approach will impact directly terrestrial ecotoxicity 1.4-DCB), human carcinogenic toxicity - 3.93 kg 1.4-DCB), and freshwater eutrophication 0.04 kg P- eq). Besides, the potential impact could be found in substituting other typ- ical synthetic fertilizers like ammonium phosphate, and potassium chlo- ride. In a study made by Alba-Reyes et al. (2019), a beneficial impact is expected in all impact categories, mainly in climate change and terrestrial acidification.

Since the soil application of digestate as biofertilizer is not within the system boundaries of the present study, the positive effects are not evalu- ated (considering the soil improvement). However, heavy metal emissions, as well as pathogens and antibiotics, could impact harmfully the environ- mental profile of the process, mainly on human toxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, and terrestrial ecotoxicity. To date, there is research lack on this direction.
73: The current work gives the potential environmental impacts of different improvement strategies in a conventional fattening pig farm in Cuba, as regards the substitution of solid fodder by LF and the sustainable use of bio- gas as a substitute for fossil fuel. In an LCA approach, five different alterna- tives were compared, specifically with six impact categories. According to the results, the worse alternative was when the current number of pigs is considered (4000), and all the fodder (corn and soybean) is imported. This performance is related to higher solid fodder for pig feeding consump- tion per functional unit to guarantee the same final pig weight, along with higher feed transportation emissions of pollutants in the entire life cycle.

Using 60 t/day of FW, considering the biogas potential to substitute diesel consumption to complement the energy demand in a future scenario (8000 pigs) was found as the alternative with the best behavior, mainly attributed to lower solid fodder consumption compared with other alternatives and the substitution of fossil fuel (diesel) consumption by biogas. The avoided product approach (fertilizer and irrigation water) plays a beneficial role in all impact categories (by 27 % up to 89 %), related mainly to the reduction of emissions of NH3 and NO2, associated with acidification and particulate matter formation. Feed production is identified as the main con- tributor in a harmful way in all impact categories studied, in a range of 8 % up to 71 %, depending on the category under assessment. A sensibility analysis is made according to the change in pig diet composition. It was obtained that LF composition is very sensible for the impact catego- ries studied. Global warming was the impact category more sensible, in contrast with terrestrial ecotoxicity with lower sensitivity for the values assessed.
